data.verse_text: Day after day they pour forth speech; night after night they reveal knowledge.
data.commentary_text: uttereth--pours forth as a stream; a perpetual testimony.

data.verse_text: Without speech or language, without a sound to be heard,
data.commentary_text: Though there is no articulate speech or words, yet without these their voice is heard (compare Margin).

data.verse_text: their voice has gone out into all the earth, their words to the ends of the world. In the heavens He has pitched a tent for the sun.
data.commentary_text: Their line--or, "instruction"--the influence exerted by their tacit display of God's perfections. Paul (Rom 10:8), quoting from the Septuagint, uses "sound," which gives the same sense.

data.verse_text: Like a bridegroom emerging from his chamber, like a champion rejoicing to run his course, it rises at one end of the heavens and runs its circuit to the other; nothing is deprived of its warmth.
data.commentary_text: The sun, as the most glorious heavenly body, is specially used to illustrate the sentiment; and his vigorous, cheerful, daily, and extensive course, and his reviving heat (including light), well display the wondrous wisdom of his Maker.

data.verse_text: The Law of the LORD is perfect, reviving the soul; the testimony of the LORD is trustworthy, making wise the simple. The precepts of the LORD are right, bringing joy to the heart; the commandments of the LORD are radiant, giving light to the eyes. The fear of the LORD is pure, enduring forever; the judgments of the LORD are true, being altogether righteous. They are more precious than gold, than much pure gold; they are sweeter than honey, than honey from the comb. By them indeed Your servant is warned; in keeping them is great reward.
data.commentary_text: The law is described by six names, epithets, and effects. It is a rule, God's testimony for the truth, His special and general prescription of duty, fear (as its cause) and judicial decision. It is distinct and certain, reliable, right, pure, holy, and true. Hence it revives those depressed by doubts, makes wise the unskilled (Ti2 3:15), rejoices the lover of truth, strengthens the desponding (Psa 13:4; Psa 34:6), provides permanent principles of conduct, and by God's grace brings a rich reward.

data.verse_text: Who can discern his own errors? Cleanse me from my hidden faults. Keep Your servant also from willful sins; may they not rule over me. Then I will be blameless and cleansed of great transgression. May the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be pleasing in Your sight, O LORD, my Rock and my Redeemer.
data.commentary_text:
The clearer our view of the law, the more manifest are our sins. Still for its full effect we need divine grace to show us our faults, acquit us, restrain us from the practice, and free us from the power, of sin. Thus only can our conduct be blameless, and our words and thoughts acceptable to God.




Next: Psalms Chapter 20

data.verse_text: May the LORD answer you in the day of trouble; may the name of the God of Jacob protect you.
data.commentary_text:
David probably composed this Psalm to express the prayers of the pious for his success as at once the head of the Church and nation. Like other compositions of which David in such relations is the subject, its sentiments have a permanent value--the prosperity of Christ's kingdom being involved, as well as typified, in that of Israel and its king. (Psa 20:1-9)

hear thee--graciously (Psa 4:1). 

name of--or manifested perfections, as power, wisdom, &c. 

defend thee--set thee on high from danger (Psa 9:9; Psa 18:3).

data.verse_text: May He send you help from the sanctuary and sustain you from Zion.
data.commentary_text: strengthen thee--sustain in conflict; even physical benefits may be included, as courage for war, &c., as such may proceed from a sense of divine favor, secured in the use of spiritual privileges.

data.verse_text: May He remember all your gifts and look favorably on your burnt offerings. Selah
data.commentary_text:
all thy offerings--or gifts, vegetable offerings. 

accept--literally, "turn to ashes" (compare Kg1 18:38). 

Selah--(See on Psa 3:2).

data.verse_text: May He give you the desires of your heart and make all your plans succeed.
data.commentary_text: thy counsel--or plan.

data.verse_text: May we shout for joy at your victory and raise a banner in the name of our God. May the LORD grant all your petitions.
data.commentary_text:
salvation--that wrought and experienced by him. 

set up our banners-- (Num 2:3, Num 2:10). In usual sense, or, as some render, "may we be made great."

data.verse_text: Now I know that the LORD saves His anointed; He answers him from His holy heaven with the saving power of His right hand.
data.commentary_text:
He speaks as if suddenly assured of a hearing. 

his anointed--not only David personally, but as the specially appointed head of His Church. 

his holy heaven--or, literally, "the heavens of His holiness," where He resides (Psa 2:6; Psa 11:4). 

saving . . . hand--His power which brings salvation.

data.verse_text: Some trust in chariots and others in horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD our God.
data.commentary_text: remember--or cause to remember, mention thankfully (Sa1 17:45; Psa 33:16).

data.verse_text: They collapse and fall, but we rise up and stand firm.
data.commentary_text:
They--that is, who trust in horses, &c. 

stand upright--literally, "we have straightened ourselves up from our distress and fears."

data.verse_text: O LORD, save the king. Answer us on the day we call.
data.commentary_text:
let the king hear--as God's representative, delivered to deliver. Perhaps a better sense is, "LORD, save the king; hear us when we call," or pray.




Next: Psalms Chapter 21

data.verse_text: O LORD, the king rejoices in Your strength. How greatly he exults in Your salvation!
data.commentary_text:
The pious are led by the Psalmist to celebrate God's favor to the king in the already conferred and in prospective victories. The doxology added may relate to both Psalms; the preceding of petition, chiefly this of thanksgiving, ascribing honor to God for His display of grace and power to His Church in all ages, not only under David, but also under his last greatest successor, "the King of the Jews." (Psa 21:1-13)

thy strength . . . thy salvation--as supplied by Thee.

data.verse_text: You have granted his heart’s desire and have not withheld the request of his lips. Selah
data.commentary_text: The sentiment affirmed in the first clause is reaffirmed by the negation of its opposite in the second.

data.verse_text: For You welcomed him with rich blessings; You placed on his head a crown of pure gold.
data.commentary_text:
preventest--literally, "to meet here in good sense," or "friendship" (Psa 59:10; compare opposite, Psa 17:13). 

blessings of goodness--which confer happiness. 

crown of pure gold--a figure for the highest royal prosperity.

data.verse_text: He asked You for life, and You granted it— length of days, forever and ever. Great is his glory in Your salvation; You bestow on him splendor and majesty.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Sa2 7:13-16). The glory and blessedness of the king as head of his line, including Christ, as well as in being God's specially selected servant, exceeded that of all others.

data.verse_text: For You grant him blessings forever; You cheer him with joy in Your presence.
data.commentary_text:
made him most blessed--or set him "to be blessings," as Abraham (Gen 12:2). 

with thy countenance--by sight of thee (Psa 16:11), or by Thy favor expressed by the light of Thy countenance (Num 6:25), or both.

data.verse_text: For the king trusts in the LORD; through the loving devotion of the Most High, he will not be shaken.
data.commentary_text: The mediate cause is the king's faith, the efficient, God's mercy.

data.verse_text: Your hand will apprehend all Your enemies; Your right hand will seize those who hate You.
data.commentary_text:
The address is now made to the king. 

hand--denotes power, and 

right hand--a more active and efficient degree of its exercise. 

find out--reach, lay hold of, indicating success in pursuit of his enemies.

data.verse_text: You will place them in a fiery furnace at the time of Your appearing. In His wrath the LORD will engulf them, and the fire will consume them.
data.commentary_text:
The king is only God's agent. 

anger--literally, "face," as appearing against them. 

as a fiery oven--as in it.

data.verse_text: You will wipe their descendants from the earth, and their offspring from the sons of men.
data.commentary_text: fruit--children (Psa 37:25; Hos 9:16).

data.verse_text: Though they intend You harm, the schemes they devise will not prevail.
data.commentary_text: This terrible overthrow, reaching to posterity, is due to their crimes (Exo 20:5-6).

data.verse_text: For You will put them to flight when Your bow is trained upon them.
data.commentary_text:
turn their back--literally, "place them [as to the] shoulder." 

against the face of them--The shooting against their faces would cause them to turn their backs in flight.

data.verse_text: Be exalted, O LORD, in Your strength; we will sing and praise Your power.
data.commentary_text:
The glory of all is ascribable to God alone.




Next: Psalms Chapter 22

data.verse_text: My God, my God, why have You forsaken me? Why are You so far from saving me, so far from my words of groaning?
data.commentary_text:
The obscure words Aijeleth Shahar in this title have various explanations. Most interpreters agree in translating them by "hind of the morning." But great difference exists as to the meaning of these words. By some they are supposed (compare Psa 9:1) to be the name of the tune to which the words of the Psalm were set; by others, the name of a musical instrument. Perhaps the best view is to regard the phrase as enigmatically expressive of the subject--the sufferer being likened to a hind pursued by hunters in the early morning (literally, "the dawn of day")--or that, while hind suggests the idea of a meek, innocent sufferer, the addition of morning denotes relief obtained. The feelings of a pious sufferer in sorrow and deliverance are vividly portrayed. He earnestly pleads for divine aid on the ground of his relation to God, whose past goodness to His people encourages hope, and then on account of the imminent danger by which he is threatened. The language of complaint is turned to that of rejoicing in the assured prospect of relief from suffering and triumph over his enemies. The use of the words of the first clause of Psa 22:1 by our Saviour on the cross, and the quotation of Psa 22:18 by John (Joh 19:24), and of Psa 22:22 by Paul (Heb 2:12), as fulfilled in His history, clearly intimate the prophetical and Messianic purport of the Psalm. The intensity of the grief, and the completeness and glory of the deliverance and triumph, alike appear to be unsuitable representations of the fortunes of any less personage. In a general and modified sense (see on Psa 16:1), the experience here detailed may be adapted to the case of all Christians suffering from spiritual foes, and delivered by divine aid, inasmuch as Christ in His human nature was their head and representative. (Psa. 22:1-31)

A summary of the complaint. Desertion by God, when overwhelmed by distress, is the climax of the sufferer's misery. 

words of my roaring--shows that the complaint is expressed intelligently, though the term "roaring" is figurative, taken from the conduct of irrational creatures in pain.

data.verse_text: I cry out by day, O my God, but You do not answer, and by night, but I have no rest.
data.commentary_text:
The long distress is evinced by-- 

am not silent--literally, "not silence to me," either meaning, I continually cry; or, corresponding with "thou hearest not," or answerest not, it may mean, there is no rest or quiet to me.

data.verse_text: Yet You are holy, enthroned on the praises of Israel.
data.commentary_text:
Still he not only refrains from charging God foolishly, but evinces his confidence in God by appealing to Him. 

thou art holy--or possessed of all the attributes which encourage trust, and the right object of the praises of the Church: hence the sufferer need not despair.

data.verse_text: In You our fathers trusted; they trusted and You delivered them. They cried out to You and were set free; they trusted in You and were not disappointed.
data.commentary_text: Past experience of God's people is a ground of trust. The mention of "our fathers" does not destroy the applicability of the words as the language of our Saviour's human nature.

data.verse_text: But I am a worm and not a man, scorned by men and despised by the people.
data.commentary_text: He who was despised and rejected of His own people, as a disgrace to the nation, might well use these words of deep abasement, which express not His real, but esteemed, value.

data.verse_text: All who see me mock me; they sneer and shake their heads:
data.commentary_text:
For the Jews used one of the gestures (Mat 27:39) here mentioned, when taunting Him on the cross, and (Mat 27:43) reproached Him almost in the very, language of this passage. 

shoot out--or, "open." 

the lip--(Compare Psa 35:21).

data.verse_text: “He trusts in the LORD, let the LORD deliver him; let the LORD rescue him, since He delights in him.”
data.commentary_text: trusted on the Lord--literally, "rolled"--that is, his burden (Psa 37:5; Pro 16:3) on the Lord. This is the language of enemies sporting with his faith in the hour of his desertion.

data.verse_text: Yet You brought me forth from the womb; You made me secure at my mother’s breast. From birth I was cast upon You; from my mother’s womb You have been my God.
data.commentary_text:
Though ironically spoken, the exhortation to trust was well founded on his previous experience of divine aid, the special illustration of which is drawn from the period of helpless infancy. 

didst make me hope--literally, "made me secure."

data.verse_text: Be not far from me, for trouble is near and there is no one to help.
data.commentary_text: From this statement of reasons for the appeal, he renews it, pleading his double extremity, the nearness of trouble, and the absence of a helper.

data.verse_text: Many bulls surround me; strong bulls of Bashan encircle me. They open their jaws against me like lions that roar and maul.
data.commentary_text: His enemies, with the vigor of bulls and rapacity of lions, surround him, eagerly seeking his ruin. The force of both figures is greater without the use of any particle denoting comparison.

data.verse_text: I am poured out like water, and all my bones are disjointed. My heart is like wax; it melts away within me.
data.commentary_text: Utter exhaustion and hopeless weakness, in these circumstances of pressing danger, are set forth by the most expressive figures; the solidity of the body is destroyed, and it becomes like water; the bones are parted; the heart, the very seat of vitality, melts like wax; all the juices of the system are dried up; the tongue can no longer perform its office, but lies parched and stiffened (compare Gen 49:4; Sa2 14:14; Psa 58:8). In this, God is regarded as the ultimate source, and men as the instruments.

data.verse_text: My strength is dried up like a potsherd, and my tongue sticks to the roof of my mouth. You lay me in the dust of death.
data.commentary_text: the dust of death--of course, denotes the grave. We need not try to find the exact counterpart of each item of the description in the particulars of our Saviour's sufferings. Figurative language resembles pictures of historical scenes, presenting substantial truth, under illustrations, which, though not essential to the facts, are not inconsistent with them. Were any portion of Christ's terrible sufferings specially designed, it was doubtless that of the garden of Gethsemane.

data.verse_text: For dogs surround me; a band of evil men encircles me; they have pierced my hands and feet.
data.commentary_text: Evildoers are well described as dogs, which, in the East, herding together, wild and rapacious, are justly objects of great abhorrence. The last clause has been a subject of much discussion (involving questions as to the genuineness of the Hebrew word translated "pierce)" which cannot be made intelligible to the English reader. Though not quoted in the New Testament, the remarkable aptness of the description to the facts of the Saviour's history, together with difficulties attending any other mode of explaining the clause in the Hebrew, justify an adherence to the terms of our version and their obvious meaning.

data.verse_text: I can count all my bones; they stare and gloat over me.
data.commentary_text: His emaciated frame, itself an item of his misery, is rendered more so as the object of delighted contemplation to his enemies. The verbs, "look" and "stare," often occur as suggestive of feelings of satisfaction (compare Psa 27:13; Psa 54:7; Psa 118:7).

data.verse_text: They divide my garments among them and cast lots for my clothing.
data.commentary_text: This literally fulfilled prediction closes the sad picture of the exposed and deserted sufferer.

data.verse_text: But You, O LORD, be not far off; O my Strength, come quickly to help me.
data.commentary_text: He now turns with unabated desire and trust to God, who, in His strength and faithfulness, is contrasted with the urgent dangers described.

data.verse_text: Deliver my soul from the sword, my precious life from the power of wild dogs.
data.commentary_text:
my soul--or self (compare Psa 3:2; Psa 16:10). 

my darling--literally, "my only one," or, "solitary one," as desolate and afflicted (Psa 25:16; Psa 35:17).

data.verse_text: Save me from the mouth of the lion; at the horns of the wild oxen You have answered me!
data.commentary_text:
Deliverance pleaded in view of former help, when in the most imminent danger, from the most powerful enemy, represented by the unicorn or wild buffalo. 

the lion's mouth--(Compare Psa 22:13). The lion often used as a figure representing violent enemies; the connecting of the mouth intimates their rapacity.

data.verse_text: I will proclaim Your name to my brothers; I will praise You in the assembly. You who fear the LORD, praise Him! All descendants of Jacob, honor Him! All offspring of Israel, revere Him! For He has not despised or detested the torment of the afflicted. He has not hidden His face from him, but has attended to his cry for help.
data.commentary_text: He declares his purpose to celebrate God's gracious dealings and publish His manifested perfections ("name," Psa 5:11), &c., and forthwith he invites the pious (those who have a reverential fear of God) to unite in special praise for a deliverance, illustrating God's kind regard for the lowly, whom men neglect [Psa 22:24]. To hide the face (or eyes) expresses a studied neglect of one's cause, and refusal of aid or sympathy (compare Psa 30:7; Isa 1:15).

data.verse_text: My praise for You resounds in the great assembly; I will fulfill my vows before those who fear You. The poor will eat and be satisfied; those who seek the LORD will praise Him. May your hearts live forever!
data.commentary_text: My praise shall be of thee--or, perhaps better, "from thee," that is, God gives grace to praise Him. With offering praise, he further evinces his gratitude by promising the payment of his vows, in celebrating the usual festival, as provided in the law (Deu 12:18; Deu 16:11), of which the pious or humble, and they that seek the Lord (His true worshippers) shall partake abundantly, and join him in praise [Psa 22:26]. In the enthusiasm produced by his lively feelings, he addresses such in words, assuring them of God's perpetual favor [Psa 22:26]. The dying of the heart denotes death (Sa1 25:37); so its living denotes life.

data.verse_text: All the ends of the earth will remember and turn to the LORD. All the families of the nations will bow down before Him. For dominion belongs to the LORD and He rules over the nations. All the rich of the earth will feast and worship; all who go down to the dust will kneel before Him— even those unable to preserve their lives.
data.commentary_text: His case illustrates God's righteous government. Beyond the existing time and people, others shall be brought to acknowledge and worship God; the fat ones, or the rich as well as the poor, the helpless who cannot keep themselves alive, shall together unite in celebrating God's delivering power, and transmit to unborn people the records of His grace.

data.verse_text: Posterity will serve Him; they will declare the Lord to a new generation.
data.commentary_text: it shall be accounted to the Lord for, &c.--or, "it shall be told of the Lord to a generation." God's wonderful works shall be told from generation to generation.

data.verse_text: They will come and proclaim His righteousness to a people yet unborn— all that He has done.
data.commentary_text:
that he hath done this--supply "it," or "this"--that is, what the Psalm has unfolded.




Next: Psalms Chapter 23

data.verse_text: The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want.
data.commentary_text:
Under a metaphor borrowed from scenes of pastoral life, with which David was familiar, he describes God's providential care in providing refreshment, guidance, protection, and abundance, and so affording grounds of confidence in His perpetual favor. (Psa 23:1-6)

Christ's relation to His people is often represented by the figure of a shepherd (Joh 10:14; Heb 13:20; Pe1 2:25; Pe1 5:4), and therefore the opinion that He is the Lord here so described, and in Gen 48:15; Psa 80:1; Isa 40:11, is not without some good reason.

data.verse_text: He makes me lie down in green pastures; He leads me beside quiet waters.
data.commentary_text:
green pastures--or, "pastures of tender grass," are mentioned, not in respect to food, but as places of cool and refreshing rest. 

the still waters--are, literally, "waters of "stillness," whose quiet flow invites to repose. They are contrasted with boisterous streams on the one hand, and stagnant, offensive pools on the other.

data.verse_text: He restores my soul; He guides me in the paths of righteousness for the sake of His name.
data.commentary_text:
To restore the soul is to revive or quicken it (Psa 19:7), or relieve it (Lam 1:11, Lam 1:19). 

paths of righteousness--those of safety, as directed by God, and pleasing to Him. 

for his name's sake--or, regard for His perfections, pledged for His people's welfare.

data.verse_text: Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for You are with me; Your rod and Your staff, they comfort me.
data.commentary_text:
In the darkest and most trying hour God is near. 

the valley of the shadow of death--is a ravine overhung by high precipitous cliffs, filled with dense forests, and well calculated to inspire dread to the timid, and afford a covert to beasts of prey. While expressive of any great danger or cause of terror, it does not exclude the greatest of all, to which it is most popularly applied, and which its terms suggest. 

thy rod and thy staff--are symbols of a shepherd's office. By them he guides his sheep.

data.verse_text: You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies. You anoint my head with oil; my cup overflows. Surely goodness and mercy will follow me all the days of my life, and I will dwell in the house of the LORD forever.
data.commentary_text:
Another figure expresses God's provided care. 

a table--or, "food," anointing 

oil--the symbol of gladness, and the overflowing 

cup--which represents abundance--are prepared for the child of God, who may feast in spite of his enemies, confident that this favor will ever attend him. This beautiful Psalm most admirably sets before us, in its chief figure, that of a shepherd, the gentle, kind, and sure care extended to God's people, who, as a shepherd, both rules and feeds them. The closing verse shows that the blessings mentioned are spiritual.




Next: Psalms Chapter 24

data.verse_text: The earth is the LORD’s, and the fullness thereof, the world and all who dwell therein.
data.commentary_text:
God's supreme sovereignty requires a befitting holiness of life and heart in His worshippers; a sentiment sublimely illustrated by describing His entrance into the sanctuary, by the symbol of His worship--the ark, as requiring the most profound homage to the glory of His Majesty. (Psa 24:1-10)

fulness--everything. 

world--the habitable globe, with 

they that dwell--forming a parallel expression to the first clause.

data.verse_text: For He has founded it upon the seas and established it upon the waters.
data.commentary_text: Poetically represents the facts of Gen 1:9.

data.verse_text: Who may ascend the hill of the LORD? Who may stand in His holy place?
data.commentary_text:
The form of a question gives vivacity. Hands, tongue, and heart are organs of action, speech, and feeling, which compose character. 

hill of the Lord--(compare Psa 2:6, &c.). His Church--the true or invisible, as typified by the earthly sanctuary.

data.verse_text: He who has clean hands and a pure heart, who does not lift up his soul to an idol or swear deceitfully.
data.commentary_text:
lifted up his soul--is to set the affections (Psa 25:1) on an object; here, 

vanity--or, any false thing, of which swearing falsely, or to falsehood, is a specification.

data.verse_text: He will receive blessing from the LORD and vindication from the God of his salvation.
data.commentary_text: righteousness--the rewards which God bestows on His people, or the grace to secure those rewards as well as the result.

data.verse_text: Such is the generation of those who seek Him, who seek Your face, O God of Jacob. Selah
data.commentary_text: Jacob--By "Jacob," we may understand God's people (compare Isa 43:22; Isa 44:2, &c.), corresponding to "the generation," as if he had said, "those who seek Thy face are Thy chosen people."

data.verse_text: Lift up your heads, O gates! Be lifted up, O ancient doors, that the King of Glory may enter! Who is this King of Glory? The LORD strong and mighty, the LORD mighty in battle. Lift up your heads, O gates! Be lifted up, O ancient doors, that the King of Glory may enter!
data.commentary_text: The entrance of the ark, with the attending procession, into the holy sanctuary is pictured to us. The repetition of the terms gives emphasis.

data.verse_text: Who is He, this King of Glory? The LORD of Hosts— He is the King of Glory. Selah
data.commentary_text:
Lord of hosts--or fully, Lord God of hosts (Hos 12:5; Amo 4:13), describes God by a title indicative of supremacy over all creatures, and especially the heavenly armies (Jos 5:14; Kg1 22:19). Whether, as some think, the actual enlargement of the ancient gates of Jerusalem be the basis of the figure, the effect of the whole is to impress us with a conception of the matchless majesty of God.




Next: Psalms Chapter 25

data.verse_text: To You, O LORD, I lift up my soul;
data.commentary_text:
The general tone of this Psalm is that of prayer for help from enemies. Distress, however, exciting a sense of sin, humble confession, supplication for pardon, preservation from sin, and divine guidance, are prominent topics. (Psa. 25:1-22)

lift up my soul-- (Psa 24:4; Psa 86:4), set my affections (compare Col 3:2).

data.verse_text: in You, my God, I trust. Do not let me be put to shame; do not let my enemies exult over me.
data.commentary_text: not be ashamed--by disappointment of hopes of relief.

data.verse_text: Surely none who wait for You will be put to shame; but those who are faithless without cause will be disgraced.
data.commentary_text: The prayer generalized as to all who wait on God--that is, who expect His favor. On the other hand, the disappointment of the perfidious, who, unprovoked, have done evil, is invoked (compare Sa2 22:9).

data.verse_text: Show me Your ways, O LORD; teach me Your paths. Guide me in Your truth and teach me, for You are the God of my salvation; all day long I wait for You.
data.commentary_text: On the ground of former favor, he invokes divine guidance, according to God's gracious ways of dealing and faithfulness.

data.verse_text: Remember, O LORD, Your compassion and loving devotion, for they are from age to age. Remember not the sins of my youth, nor my rebellious acts; remember me according to Your loving devotion, because of Your goodness, O LORD.
data.commentary_text: Confessing past and present sins, he pleads for mercy, not on palliations of sin, but on God's well-known benevolence.

data.verse_text: Good and upright is the LORD; therefore He shows sinners the way.
data.commentary_text:
upright--acting according to His promise. 

sinners--the general term, limited by the 

meek--who are penitent. 

the way--and his way--God's way of providence.

data.verse_text: He guides the humble in what is right and teaches them His way.
data.commentary_text: in judgment--rightly.

data.verse_text: All the LORD’s ways are loving and faithful to those who keep His covenant and His decrees.
data.commentary_text:
paths--similar sense--His modes of dealing (compare Psa 25:4). 

mercy and truth-- (Job 14:1-22), God's grace in promising and faithfulness in performing.

data.verse_text: For the sake of Your name, O LORD, forgive my iniquity, for it is great.
data.commentary_text: God's perfections of love, mercy, goodness, and truth are manifested (his name, compare Psa 9:10) in pardoning sin, and the greatness of sin renders pardon more needed.

data.verse_text: Who is the man who fears the LORD? He will instruct him in the path chosen for him.
data.commentary_text: What he asks for himself is the common lot of all the pious.

data.verse_text: His soul will dwell in prosperity, and his descendants will inherit the land.
data.commentary_text: inherit the earth--(compare Mat 5:5). The phrase, alluding to the promise of Canaan, expresses all the blessings included in that promise, temporal as well as spiritual.

data.verse_text: The LORD confides in those who fear Him, and reveals His covenant to them.
data.commentary_text: The reason of the blessing explained--the pious enjoy communion with God (compare Pro 3:21, Pro 3:12), and, of course, learn His gracious terms of pardon.

data.verse_text: My eyes are always on the LORD, for He will free my feet from the mesh.
data.commentary_text:
His trust in God is fixed. 

net--is frequently used as a figure for dangers by enemies (Psa 9:15; Psa 10:9).

data.verse_text: Turn to me and be gracious, for I am lonely and afflicted. The troubles of my heart increase; free me from my distress. Consider my affliction and trouble, and take away all my sins. Consider my enemies, for they are many, and they hate me with vicious hatred.
data.commentary_text: A series of earnest appeals for aid because God had seemed to desert him (compare Psa 13:1; Psa 17:13, &c.), his sins oppressed him, his enemies had enlarged his troubles and were multiplied, increasing in hate and violence (Psa 9:8; Psa 18:48).

data.verse_text: Guard my soul and deliver me; let me not be put to shame, for I take refuge in You.
data.commentary_text:
keep my soul-- (Psa 16:1). 

put my trust--flee for refuge (Psa 2:12).

data.verse_text: May integrity and uprightness preserve me, because I wait for You.
data.commentary_text:
In conscious innocence of the faults charged by his enemies, he confidently commits his cause to God. Some refer-- 

integrity, &c.--to God, meaning His covenant faithfulness. This sense, though good, is an unusual application of the terms.

data.verse_text: Redeem Israel, O God, from all its distress.
data.commentary_text:
Extend these blessings to all Thy people in all their distresses.




Next: Psalms Chapter 26

data.verse_text: Vindicate me, O LORD! For I have walked with integrity; I have trusted in the LORD without wavering.
data.commentary_text:
After appealing to God's judgment on his avowed integrity and innocence of the charges laid by his enemies, the Psalmist professes delight in God's worship, and prays for exemption from the fate of the wicked, expressing assurance of God's favor. (Psa 26:1-12)

Judge--decide on my case; the appeal of innocence. 

in mine integrity--freedom from blemish (compare Psa 25:21). His confidence of perseverance results from trust in God's sustaining grace.

data.verse_text: Test me, O LORD, and try me; examine my heart and mind.
data.commentary_text: He asks the most careful scrutiny of his affections and thoughts (Psa 7:9), or motives.

data.verse_text: For Your loving devotion is before my eyes, and I have walked in Your truth.
data.commentary_text: As often, the ground of prayer for present help is former favor.

data.verse_text: I do not sit with deceitful men, nor keep company with hypocrites. I hate the mob of evildoers, and refuse to sit with the wicked.
data.commentary_text: As exemplified by the fruits of divine grace, presented in his life, especially in his avoiding the wicked and his purposes of cleaving to God's worship.

data.verse_text: I wash my hands in innocence that I may go about Your altar, O LORD, to raise my voice in thanksgiving and declare all Your wonderful works.
data.commentary_text: wash mine hands--expressive symbol of freedom from sinful acts (compare Mat 27:24).

data.verse_text: O LORD, I love the house where You dwell, the place where Your glory resides.
data.commentary_text:
the habitation of thy house--where Thy house rests, as the tabernacle was not yet permanently fixed. 

honour dwelleth--conveys an allusion to the Holy of Holies.

data.verse_text: Do not take my soul away with sinners, or my life with men of bloodshed,
data.commentary_text:
Gather not, &c.--Bring me not to death. 

bloody men--(compare Psa 5:6).

data.verse_text: in whose hands are wicked schemes, whose right hands are full of bribes.
data.commentary_text: Their whole conduct is that of violence and fraud.

data.verse_text: But I will walk with integrity; redeem me and be merciful to me.
data.commentary_text: But, &c.--He contrasts his character and destiny with that of the wicked (compare Psa 26:1-2).

data.verse_text: My feet stand on level ground; in the congregations I will bless the LORD.
data.commentary_text:
even place--free from occasions of stumbling--safety in his course is denoted. Hence he will render to God his praise publicly.




Next: Psalms Chapter 27

data.verse_text: The LORD is my light and my salvation— whom shall I fear? The LORD is the stronghold of my life— whom shall I dread?
data.commentary_text:
With a general strain of confidence, hope, and joy, especially in God's worship, in the midst of dangers, the Psalmist introduces prayer for divine help and guidance. (Psa 27:1-14)

light--is a common figure for comfort. 

strength--or, "stronghold"--affording security against all violence. The interrogations give greater vividness to the negation implied.

data.verse_text: When the wicked came upon me to devour my flesh, my enemies and foes stumbled and fell.
data.commentary_text:
eat . . . my flesh-- (Job 19:22; Psa 14:4). The allusion to wild beasts illustrates their rapacity. 

they stumbled--"they" is emphatic; not I, but they were destroyed.

data.verse_text: Though an army encamps around me, my heart will not fear; though a war breaks out against me, I will keep my trust.
data.commentary_text:
In the greatest dangers. 

in this--that is, then, in such extremity.

data.verse_text: One thing I have asked of the LORD; this is what I desire: to dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life, to gaze on the beauty of the LORD and seek Him in His temple. For in the day of trouble He will hide me in His shelter; He will conceal me under the cover of His tent; He will set me high upon a rock.
data.commentary_text: The secret of his confidence is his delight in communion with God (Psa 16:11; Psa 23:6), beholding the harmony of His perfections, and seeking His favor in His temple or palace; a term applicable to the tabernacle (compare Psa 5:7). There he is safe (Psa 31:21; Psa 61:5). The figure is changed in the last clause, but the sentiment is the same.

data.verse_text: Then my head will be held high above my enemies around me. At His tabernacle I will offer sacrifices with shouts of joy; I will sing and make music to the LORD.
data.commentary_text: head be lifted up--I shall be placed beyond the reach of my enemies. Hence he avows his purpose of rendering joyful thank offerings.

data.verse_text: Hear, O LORD, my voice when I call; be merciful and answer me.
data.commentary_text:
Still pressing need extorts prayer for help. 

cry with my voice--denotes earnestness. Other things equal, Christians in earnest pray audibly, even in secret.

data.verse_text: My heart said, “Seek His face.” Your face, O LORD, I will seek.
data.commentary_text: The meaning is clear, though the construction in a literal translation is obscure. The English Version supplies the implied clause. To seek God's face is to seek His favor (Psa 105:4).

data.verse_text: Hide not Your face from me, nor turn away Your servant in anger. You have been my helper; do not leave me or forsake me, O God of my salvation.
data.commentary_text: Hide not, &c.-- (Psa 4:6; Psa 22:24). Against rejection he pleads former mercy and love.

data.verse_text: Though my father and mother forsake me, the LORD will receive me.
data.commentary_text: In the extremity of earthly destitution (Psa 31:11; Psa 38:11), God provides (compare Mat 25:35).

data.verse_text: Teach me Your way, O LORD, and lead me on a level path, because of my oppressors.
data.commentary_text:
thy way--of providence. 

a plain path-- (Psa 26:12). 

enemies--literally, "watchers for my fall" (Psa 5:8).

data.verse_text: Do not hand me over to the will of my foes, for false witnesses rise up against me, breathing out violence.
data.commentary_text:
will--literally, "soul," "desire" (Psa 35:25). 

enemies--literally, "oppressors." Falsehood aids cruelty against him. 

breathe out--as being filled with it (Act 9:1).

data.verse_text: Still I am certain to see the goodness of the LORD in the land of the living.
data.commentary_text:
The strong emotion is indicated by the incomplete sentence, for which the English Version supplies a proper clause; or, omitting that, and rendering, "yet I believed," &c., the contrast of his faith and his danger is expressed. 

to see--is to experience (Psa 22:17).

data.verse_text: Wait patiently for the LORD; be strong and courageous. Wait patiently for the LORD!
data.commentary_text:
Wait, &c.--in confident expectation. The last clause is, literally, "and wait," &c., as if expecting new measures of help.




Next: Psalms Chapter 28

data.verse_text: To You, O LORD, I call; be not deaf to me, O my Rock. For if You remain silent, I will be like those descending to the Pit.
data.commentary_text:
An earnest cry for divine aid against his enemies, as being also those of God, is followed by the Psalmist's praise in assurance of a favorable answer, and a prayer for all God's people. (Psa 28:1-9)

my rock-- (Psa 18:2, Psa 18:31). 

be not silent to me--literally, "from me," deaf or inattentive. 

become like them, &c.--share their fate. 

go down into the pit--or, "grave" (Psa 30:3).

data.verse_text: Hear my cry for mercy when I call to You for help, when I lift up my hands toward Your holy sanctuary.
data.commentary_text:
lift up my hands--a gesture of prayer (Psa 63:4; Psa 141:2). 

oracle--place of speaking (Exo 25:22; Num 7:89), where God answered His people (compare Psa 5:7).

data.verse_text: Do not drag me away with the wicked, and with the workers of iniquity, who speak peace to their neighbors while malice is in their hearts.
data.commentary_text: Draw me not away--implies punishment as well as death (compare Psa 26:9). Hypocrisy is the special wickedness mentioned.

data.verse_text: Repay them according to their deeds and for their works of evil. Repay them for what their hands have done; bring back on them what they deserve.
data.commentary_text:
The imprecation is justified in Psa 28:5. The force of the passage is greatly enhanced by the accumulation of terms describing their sin. 

endeavours--points out their deliberate sinfulness.

data.verse_text: Since they show no regard for the works of the LORD or what His hands have done, He will tear them down and never rebuild them.
data.commentary_text:
Disregard of God's judgments brings a righteous punishment. 

destroy . . . build . . . up--The positive strengthened by the negative form.

data.verse_text: Blessed be the LORD, for He has heard my cry for mercy.
data.commentary_text: supplications--or, "cries for mercy."

data.verse_text: The LORD is my strength and my shield; my heart trusts in Him, and I am helped. Therefore my heart rejoices, and I give thanks to Him with my song.
data.commentary_text: The repetition of "heart" denotes his sincerity.

data.verse_text: The LORD is the strength of His people, a stronghold of salvation for His anointed.
data.commentary_text:
The distinction made between the people. 

their strength--and the anointed--may indicate Absalom's rebellion as the occasion.

data.verse_text: Save Your people and bless Your inheritance; shepherd them and carry them forever.
data.commentary_text:
The special prayer for the people sustains this view. 

feed them--as a shepherd (Psa 23:1, &c.).




Next: Psalms Chapter 29

data.verse_text: Ascribe to the LORD, O heavenly beings, ascribe to the LORD glory and strength.
data.commentary_text:
Trust in God is encouraged by the celebration of His mighty power as illustrated in His dominion over the natural world, in some of its most terrible and wonderful exhibitions. (Psa 29:1-11)

Give--or, "ascribe" (Deu 32:3). 

mighty--or, "sons of the mighty" (Psa 89:6). Heavenly beings, as angels.

data.verse_text: Ascribe to the LORD the glory due His name; worship the LORD in the splendor of His holiness.
data.commentary_text:
name--as (Psa 5:11; Psa 8:1). 

beauty of holiness--the loveliness of a spiritual worship, of which the perceptible beauty of the sanctuary worship was but a type.

data.verse_text: The voice of the LORD is over the waters; the God of glory thunders; the LORD is heard over many waters.
data.commentary_text:
The voice of the Lord--audible exhibition of His power in the tempest, of which thunder is a specimen, but not the uniform or sole example. 

the waters--the clouds or vapors (Psa 18:11; Jer 10:13).

data.verse_text: The voice of the LORD is powerful; the voice of the LORD is majestic.
data.commentary_text: powerful . . . majesty--literally, "in power, in majesty."

data.verse_text: The voice of the LORD breaks the cedars; the LORD shatters the cedars of Lebanon. He makes Lebanon skip like a calf, and Sirion like a young wild ox.
data.commentary_text: The tall and large cedars, especially of Lebanon, are shivered, utterly broken. The waving of the mountain forests before the wind is expressed by the figure of skipping or leaping.

data.verse_text: The voice of the LORD strikes with flames of fire.
data.commentary_text: divideth--literally, "hews off." The lightning, like flakes and splinters hewed from stone or wood, flies through the air.

data.verse_text: The voice of the LORD shakes the wilderness; the LORD shakes the Wilderness of Kadesh.
data.commentary_text: the wilderness--especially Kadesh, south of Judea, is selected as another scene of this display of divine power, as a vast and desolate region impresses the mind, like mountains, with images of grandeur.

data.verse_text: The voice of the LORD twists the oaks and strips the forests bare. And in His temple all cry, “Glory!”
data.commentary_text: Terror-stricken animals and denuded forests close the illustration. In view of this scene of awful sublimity, God's worshippers respond to the call of Psa 29:2, and speak or cry, "Glory!" By "temple," or "palace" (God's residence, Psa 5:7), may here be meant heaven, or the whole frame of nature, as the angels are called on for praise.

data.verse_text: The LORD sits enthroned over the flood; the LORD is enthroned as King forever. The LORD gives His people strength; the LORD blesses His people with peace.
data.commentary_text:
Over this terrible raging of the elements God is enthroned, directing and restraining by sovereign power; and hence the comfort of His people. "This awful God is ours, our Father and our Love."




Next: Psalms Chapter 30

data.verse_text: I will exalt You, O LORD, for You have lifted me up and have not allowed my foes to rejoice over me.
data.commentary_text:
Literally, "A Psalm-Song"--a composition to be sung with musical instruments, or without them--or, "Song of the dedication," &c. specifying the particular character of the Psalm. Some suppose that of David should be connected with the name of the composition, and not with "house"; and refer for the occasion to the selection of a site for the temple (Ch1 21:26-30; Ch1 22:1). But "house" is never used absolutely for the temple, and "dedication" does not well apply to such an occasion. Though the phrase in the Hebrew, "dedication of the house of David," is an unusual form, yet it is equally unusual to disconnect the name of the author and the composition. As a "dedication of David's house" (as provided, Deu 20:5), the scope of the Psalm well corresponds with the state of repose and meditation on his past trials suited to such an occasion (Sa2 5:11; Sa2 7:2). For beginning with a celebration of God's delivering favor, in which he invites others to join, he relates his prayer in distress, and God's gracious and prompt answer. (Psa 30:1-12)

lifted me up--as one is drawn from a well (Psa 40:2).

data.verse_text: O LORD my God, I cried to You for help, and You healed me.
data.commentary_text: healed me--Affliction is often described as disease (Psa 6:2; Psa 41:4; Psa 107:20), and so relief by healing.

data.verse_text: O LORD, You pulled me up from Sheol; You spared me from descending into the Pit.
data.commentary_text:
The terms describe extreme danger. 

soul--or, "myself." 

grave--literally, "hell," as in Psa 16:10. 

hast kept me . . . pit--quickened or revived me from the state of dying (compare Psa 28:1).

data.verse_text: Sing to the LORD, O you His saints, and praise His holy name.
data.commentary_text:
remembrance--the thing remembered or memorial. 

holiness--as the sum of God's perfections (compare Psa 22:3), used as name (Exo 3:15; Psa 135:13).

data.verse_text: For His anger is fleeting, but His favor lasts a lifetime. Weeping may stay the night, but joy comes in the morning.
data.commentary_text: Relatively, the longest experience of divine anger by the pious is momentary. These precious words have consoled millions.

data.verse_text: In prosperity I said, “I will never be shaken.”
data.commentary_text: What particular prosperity is meant we do not know; perhaps his accession to the throne. In his self-complacent elation he was checked by God's hiding His face (compare Psa 22:24; Psa 27:9).

data.verse_text: O LORD, You favored me; You made my mountain stand strong. When You hid Your face, I was dismayed.
data.commentary_text: troubled--confounded with fear (Psa 2:5).

data.verse_text: To You, O LORD, I called, and I begged my Lord for mercy: “What gain is there in my bloodshed, in my descent to the Pit? Will the dust praise You? Will it proclaim Your faithfulness? Hear me, O LORD, and have mercy; O LORD, be my helper.”
data.commentary_text: As in Psa 6:5; Psa 88:10; Isa 38:18, the appeal for mercy is based on the destruction of his agency in praising God here, which death would produce. The terms expressing relief are poetical, and not to be pressed, though "dancing" is the translation of a word which means a lute, whose cheerful notes are contrasted with mourning, or (Amo 5:16) wailing.

data.verse_text: You turned my mourning into dancing; You peeled off my sackcloth and clothed me with joy,
data.commentary_text: sackcloth--was used, even by kings, in distress (Ch1 21:16; Isa 37:1) but "gladness," used for a garment, shows the language to be figurative.

data.verse_text: that my heart may sing Your praises and not be silent. O LORD my God, I will give thanks forever.
data.commentary_text:
Though "my" is supplied before "glory" it is better as in Psa 16:9, to receive it as used for tongue, the organ of praise. The ultimate end of God's mercies to us is our praise to Him.




Next: Psalms Chapter 31

data.verse_text: In You, O LORD, I have taken refuge; let me never be put to shame; save me by Your righteousness.
data.commentary_text:
The prayer of a believer in time of deep distress. In the first part, cries for help are mingled with expressions of confidence. Then the detail of griefs engrosses his attention, till, in the assurance of strong but submissive faith, he rises to the language of unmingled joyful trust and exhorts others to like love and confidence towards God. (Psa. 31:1-24)

Expresses the general tone of feeling of the Psalm.

data.verse_text: Incline Your ear to me; come quickly to my rescue. Be my rock of refuge, the stronghold of my deliverance. For You are my rock and my fortress; lead me and guide me for the sake of Your name. You free me from the net laid out for me, for You are my refuge.
data.commentary_text: He seeks help in God's righteous government (Psa 5:8), and begs for an attentive hearing, and speedy and effectual aid. With no other help and no claim of merit, he relies solely on God's regard to His own perfections for a safe guidance and release from the snares of his enemies. On the terms "rock," &c., (compare Psa 17:2; Psa 18:2, Psa 18:50; Psa 20:6; Psa 23:3; Psa 25:21).

data.verse_text: Into Your hands I commit my spirit; You have redeemed me, O LORD, God of truth. I hate those who cling to worthless idols, but in the LORD I trust.
data.commentary_text: commit my spirit--my life, or myself. Our Saviour used the words on the Cross [Luk 23:46], not as prophetical, but, as many pious men have done, as expressive of His unshaken confidence in God. The Psalmist rests on God's faithfulness to His promises to His people, and hence avows himself one of them, detesting all who revere objects of idolatry (compare Deu 32:21; Co1 8:4).

data.verse_text: I will be glad and rejoice in Your loving devotion, for You have seen my affliction; You have known the anguish of my soul.
data.commentary_text: hast known my soul, &c.--had regard to me in trouble.

data.verse_text: You have not delivered me to the enemy; You have set my feet in the open.
data.commentary_text:
shut me up . . . enemy--abandon to (Sa1 23:11). 

large room--place of safety (compare Psa 18:19).

data.verse_text: Be merciful to me, O LORD, for I am in distress; my eyes fail from sorrow, my soul and body as well.
data.commentary_text:
mine eye, &c.--denotes extreme weakness (compare Psa 6:7). 

grief--mingled sorrow and indignation (Psa 6:7). 

soul and . . . belly--the whole person.

data.verse_text: For my life is consumed with grief and my years with groaning; my iniquity has drained my strength, and my bones are wasting away.
data.commentary_text:
Though the effects ascribed to grief are not mere figures of speech-- 

spent . . . consumed--must be taken in the modified sense of wasted and decayed. 

iniquity--or, suffering by it (see on Psa 40:12).

data.verse_text: Among all my enemies I am a disgrace, and among my neighbors even more. I am dreaded by my friends— they flee when they see me on the street.
data.commentary_text: among--or, literally, "from," or, "by" my enemies. The latter clauses describe the progress of his disgrace to the lowest degree, till,

data.verse_text: I am forgotten like a dead man, out of mind. I am like a broken vessel.
data.commentary_text: he is forgotten as one dead, and contemned as a useless broken vessel.

data.verse_text: For I hear the slander of many; there is terror on every side. They conspire against me and plot to take my life.
data.commentary_text: For--introduces further reasons for his prayer, the unjust, deliberate, and murderous purposes of his foes.

data.verse_text: But I trust in You, O LORD; I say, “You are my God.”
data.commentary_text: In his profession of trust he includes the terms of the prayer expressing it.

data.verse_text: My times are in Your hands; deliver me from my enemies and from those who pursue me.
data.commentary_text:
times--course of life. 

deliver . . . hand--opposed to "shut me up," &c., of Psa 31:8.

data.verse_text: Make Your face shine on Your servant; save me by Your loving devotion. O LORD, let me not be ashamed, for I have called on You. Let the wicked be put to shame; let them lie silent in Sheol. May lying lips be silenced— lips that speak with arrogance against the righteous, full of pride and contempt.
data.commentary_text: Make . . . shine--(Compare Num 6:25; Psa 4:6). Deprecating from himself, he imprecates on the wicked God's displeasure, and prays that their virulent persecution of him may be stopped.

data.verse_text: How great is Your goodness which You have laid up for those who fear You, which You have bestowed before the sons of men on those who take refuge in You!
data.commentary_text: God displays openly His purposed goodness to His people.

data.verse_text: You hide them in the secret place of Your presence from the schemes of men. You conceal them in Your shelter from accusing tongues. Blessed be the LORD, for He has shown me His loving devotion in a city under siege.
data.commentary_text: the secret of thy presence--or, covering of Thy countenance; the protection He thus affords; compare Psa 27:5 for a similar figure; "dwelling" used there for "presence" here. The idea of security further presented by the figure of a tent and a fortified city [Psa 31:21].

data.verse_text: In my alarm I said, “I am cut off from Your sight!” But You heard my plea for mercy when I called to You for help.
data.commentary_text:
For I said--literally, "And I said," in an adversative sense. I, thus favored, was despondent. 

in my haste--in my terror. 

cut off . . . eyes--from all the protection of Thy presence.

data.verse_text: Love the LORD, all His saints. The LORD preserves the faithful, but fully repays the arrogant. Be strong and courageous, all you who hope in the LORD.
data.commentary_text:
the Lord . . . proud doer--literally, "the Lord is keeping faith," that is, with His people, and is repaying, &c. Then let none despair, but take courage; their hopes shall not be in vain.




Next: Psalms Chapter 32

data.verse_text: Blessed is he whose transgressions are forgiven, whose sins are covered.
data.commentary_text:
Maschil--literally, "giving instruction." The Psalmist describes the blessings of His forgiveness, succeeding the pains of conviction, and deduces from his own experience instruction and exhortation to others. (Psa 32:1-11)

(Compare Rom 4:6). 

forgiven--literally, "taken away," opposed to retain (Joh 20:23). 

covered--so that God no longer regards the sin (Psa 85:3).

data.verse_text: Blessed is the man whose iniquity the LORD does not count against him, in whose spirit there is no deceit.
data.commentary_text:
imputeth--charge to him, and treat him accordingly. 

no guile--or, deceit, no false estimate of himself, nor insincerity before God (compare Rom 8:1).

data.verse_text: When I kept silent, my bones became brittle from my groaning all day long.
data.commentary_text:
A vivid description of felt, but unacknowledged, sin. 

When--literally, "for," as in Psa 32:4.

data.verse_text: For day and night Your hand was heavy upon me; my strength was drained as in the summer heat. Selah
data.commentary_text:
thy hand--of God, or power in distressing him (Psa 38:2). 

moisture--vital juices of the body, the parching heat of which expresses the anguish of the soul. On the other figures, compare Psa 6:2, Psa 6:7; Psa 31:9-11. If composed on the occasion of the fifty-first Psalm, this distress may have been protracted for several months.

data.verse_text: Then I acknowledged my sin to You and did not hide my iniquity. I said, “I will confess my transgressions to the LORD,” and You forgave the guilt of my sin. Selah
data.commentary_text: A prompt fulfilment of the purposed confession is followed by a prompt forgiveness.

data.verse_text: Therefore let all the godly pray to You while You may be found. Surely when great waters rise, they will not come near.
data.commentary_text:
For this--that is, my happy experience. 

godly--pious in the sense of Psa 4:3. 

a time-- (Isa 55:6); when God's Spirit inclines us to seek pardon, He is ready to forgive. 

floods, &c.--denotes great danger (Psa 18:17; Psa 66:12).

data.verse_text: You are my hiding place. You protect me from trouble; You surround me with songs of deliverance. Selah
data.commentary_text: His experience illustrates the statement of Psa 32:6.

data.verse_text: I will instruct you and teach you the way you should go; I will give you counsel and watch over you.
data.commentary_text:
Whether, as most likely, the language of David (compare Psa 51:13), or that of God, this is a promise of divine guidance. 

I will . . . mine eye--or, My eye shall be on thee, watching and directing thy way.

data.verse_text: Do not be like the horse or mule, which have no understanding; they must be controlled with bit and bridle to make them come to you.
data.commentary_text: The latter clause, more literally, "in that they come not near thee"; that is, because they will not come, &c., unless forced by bit and bridle.

data.verse_text: Many are the sorrows of the wicked, but loving devotion surrounds him who trusts in the LORD.
data.commentary_text: The sorrows of the impenitent contrasted with the peace and safety secured by God's mercy.

data.verse_text: Be glad in the LORD and rejoice, O righteous ones; shout for joy, all you upright in heart.
data.commentary_text:
The righteous and upright, or those conforming to the divine teaching for securing the divine blessing, may well rejoice with shouting.




Next: Psalms Chapter 33

data.verse_text: Rejoice in the LORD, O righteous ones; it is fitting for the upright to praise Him. Praise the LORD with the harp; make music to Him with ten strings.
data.commentary_text:
A call to lively and joyous praise to God for His glorious attributes and works, as displayed in creation, and His general and special providence, in view of which, the Psalmist, for all the pious, professes trust and joy and invokes God's mercy. (Psa. 33:1-22)

The sentiment falls in with Psa 32:11 (compare Co1 14:15). The instruments (Psa 92:3; Psa 144:9) do not exclude the voice.

data.verse_text: Sing to Him a new song; play skillfully with a shout of joy.
data.commentary_text:
a new song--fresh, adapted to the occasion (Psa 40:3; Psa 96:1). 

play skilfully--(Compare Sa1 16:17).

data.verse_text: For the word of the LORD is upright, and all His work is trustworthy. The LORD loves righteousness and justice; the earth is full of His loving devotion.
data.commentary_text: Reasons for praise: first, God's truth, faithfulness, and mercy, generally; then, His creative power which all must honor.

data.verse_text: By the word of the LORD the heavens were made, and all the stars by the breath of His mouth. He piles up the waters of the sea; He puts the depths into storehouses. Let all the earth fear the LORD; let all the people of the world revere Him.
data.commentary_text: In "word" and "breath"--or, "spirit," there may be an allusion to the Son (Joh 1:1) and Holy Spirit.

data.verse_text: For He spoke, and it came to be; He commanded, and it stood firm.
data.commentary_text:
he spake--literally, "said." 

it was--The addition of "done" weakens the sense (compare Gen 1:3-10).

data.verse_text: The LORD frustrates the plans of the nations; He thwarts the devices of the peoples. The counsel of the LORD stands forever, the purposes of His heart to all generations.
data.commentary_text:
In God's providence He thwarts men's purposes and executes His own. 

heathen--literally, "nations."

data.verse_text: Blessed is the nation whose God is the LORD, the people He has chosen as His inheritance!
data.commentary_text: The inference from the foregoing in Psa 33:12 is illustrated by God's special providence, underlying which is His minute knowledge of all men.

data.verse_text: The LORD looks down from heaven; He sees all the sons of men. From His dwelling place He gazes on all who inhabit the earth.
data.commentary_text: looketh--intently (Isa 14:16).

data.verse_text: He shapes the hearts of each; He considers all their works.
data.commentary_text:
fashioneth--or, "forms," and hence knows and controls (Pro 21:1). 

alike--without exception. 

considereth--or, "understands"; God knows men's motives.

data.verse_text: No king is saved by his vast army; no warrior is delivered by his great strength.
data.commentary_text: Men's usual reliances in their greatest exigencies are, in themselves, useless.

data.verse_text: A horse is a vain hope for salvation; even its great strength cannot save.
data.commentary_text:
On the war horse (compare Job 39:19-25). 

a vain thing--a lie, which deceives us.

data.verse_text: Surely the eyes of the LORD are on those who fear Him, on those whose hope is in His loving devotion to deliver them from death and keep them alive in famine.
data.commentary_text: Contrasted is God's guidance and power to save from the greatest earthly evil and its most painful precursor, and hence from all.

data.verse_text: Our soul waits for the LORD; He is our help and our shield.
data.commentary_text: waiteth--in earnest expectation.

data.verse_text: For our hearts rejoice in Him, since we trust in His holy name. May Your loving devotion rest on us, O LORD, as we put our hope in You.
data.commentary_text:
his holy name--(Compare Psa 5:12; Psa 22:22; Psa 30:4). Our faith measures mercy (Mat 9:29); and if of grace, it is no more of debt (Rom 11:6).




Next: Psalms Chapter 34

data.verse_text: I will bless the LORD at all times; His praise will always be on my lips.
data.commentary_text:
On the title compare Sa1 21:13. Abimelech was the general name of the sovereign (Gen 20:2). After celebrating God's gracious dealings with him, the Psalmist exhorts others to make trial of His providential care, instructing them how to secure it. He then contrasts God's care of His people and His punitive providence towards the wicked. (Psa. 34:1-22)

Even in distress, which excites supplication, there is always matter for praising and thanking God (compare Eph 5:20; Phi 4:6).

data.verse_text: My soul boasts in the LORD; let the oppressed hear and rejoice.
data.commentary_text:
make her boast--"glory" (Psa 105:3; compare Gal 6:14). 

humble--"the pious," as in Psa 9:12; Psa 25:9.

data.verse_text: Magnify the LORD with me; let us exalt His name together.
data.commentary_text:
magnify the Lord--ascribe greatness to Him, an act of praise. 

together--"alike" (Psa 33:15), or, equally, without exception.

data.verse_text: I sought the LORD, and He answered me; He delivered me from all my fears.
data.commentary_text: delivered . . . fears--as well as actual evil (Psa 64:1).

data.verse_text: Those who look to Him are radiant with joy; their faces shall never be ashamed.
data.commentary_text:
God's favor to the pious generally, and to himself specially, is celebrated. 

looked--with desire for help. 

lightened--or, "brightened," expressing joy, opposed to the downcast features of those who are ashamed or disappointed (Psa 25:2-3).

data.verse_text: This poor man called out, and the LORD heard him; He saved him from all his troubles.
data.commentary_text: This poor man--literally, "humble," himself as a specimen of such.

data.verse_text: The angel of the LORD encamps around those who fear Him, and he delivers them.
data.commentary_text:
angel--of the covenant (Isa 63:9), of whom as a leader of God's host (Jos 5:14; Kg1 22:19), the phrase-- 

encampeth, &c.--is appropriate; or, "angel" used collectively for angels (Heb 1:14).

data.verse_text: Taste and see that the LORD is good; blessed is the man who takes refuge in Him!
data.commentary_text: taste and see--try and experience.

data.verse_text: Fear the LORD, you His saints, for those who fear Him lack nothing.
data.commentary_text:
that fear him--who are pious--fear and love (Pro 1:7; Pro 9:10). 

saints--consecrated to His service (Isa 40:31).

data.verse_text: Young lions go lacking and hungry, but those who seek the LORD lack no good thing.
data.commentary_text: not want any good--"good" is emphatic; they may be afflicted (compare Psa 34:10); but this may be a good (Co2 4:17-18; Heb 12:10-11).

data.verse_text: Come, children, listen to me; I will teach you the fear of the LORD.
data.commentary_text: children--subjects of instruction (Pro 1:8, Pro 1:10).

data.verse_text: Who is the man who delights in life, who desires to see good days?
data.commentary_text: What man--Whoever desires the blessings of piety, let him attend.

data.verse_text: Keep your tongue from evil and your lips from deceitful speech. Turn away from evil and do good; seek peace and pursue it.
data.commentary_text: Sins of thought included in those of speech (Luk 6:45), avoiding evil and doing good in our relations to men are based on a right relation to God.

data.verse_text: The eyes of the LORD are on the righteous, and His ears are inclined to their cry.
data.commentary_text: eyes of the Lord are upon-- (Psa 32:8; Psa 33:18).

data.verse_text: But the face of the LORD is against those who do evil, to wipe out all memory of them from the earth.
data.commentary_text:
face . . . against--opposed to them (Lev 17:10; Lev 20:3). 

cut off the remembrance--utterly destroy (Psa 109:13).

data.verse_text: The righteous cry out, and the LORD hears; He delivers them from all their troubles. The LORD is near to the brokenhearted; He saves the contrite in spirit. Many are the afflictions of the righteous, but the LORD delivers him from them all.
data.commentary_text: Humble penitents are objects of God's special tender regard (Psa 51:19; Isa 57:15).

data.verse_text: He protects all his bones; not one of them will be broken.
data.commentary_text: bones--framework of the body.

data.verse_text: Evil will slay the wicked, and the haters of the righteous will be condemned. The LORD redeems His servants, and none who take refuge in Him will be condemned.
data.commentary_text:
Contrast in the destiny of righteous and wicked; the former shall be delivered and never come into condemnation (Joh 5:24; Rom 8:1); the latter are left under condemnation and desolate.




Next: Psalms Chapter 35

data.verse_text: Contend with my opponents, O LORD; fight against those who fight against me. Take up Your shield and buckler; arise and come to my aid.
data.commentary_text:
The Psalmist invokes God's aid, contrasting the hypocrisy, cunning, and malice of his enemies with his integrity and generosity. The imprecations of the first part including a brief notice of their conduct, the fuller exposition of their hypocrisy and malice in the second, and the earnest prayer for deliverance from their scornful triumph in the last, are each closed (Psa 35:9-10, Psa 35:18, Psa 35:27-28) with promises of praise for the desired relief, in which his friends will unite. The historical occasion is probably 1Sa. 24:1-22. (Psa. 35:1-28)

God is invoked in the character of a warrior (Exo 15:3; Deu 32:41).

data.verse_text: Draw the spear and javelin against my pursuers; say to my soul: “I am your salvation.”
data.commentary_text:
fight against--literally, "devour my devourers." 

stop the way against--literally, "shut up" (the way), to meet or oppose, &c. 

I . . . thy salvation--who saves thee.

data.verse_text: May those who seek my life be disgraced and put to shame; may those who plan to harm me be driven back and confounded.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Psa 9:17). 

devise my hurt--purpose for evil to me.

data.verse_text: May they be like chaff in the wind, as the angel of the LORD drives them away. May their path be dark and slick, as the angel of the LORD pursues.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Psa 1:4) --a terrible fate; driven by wind on a slippery path in darkness, and hotly pursued by supernatural violence (Sa2 24:16; Act 12:23).

data.verse_text: For without cause they laid their net for me; without reason they dug a pit for my soul. May ruin befall them by surprise; may the net they hid ensnare them; may they fall into the hazard they created. Then my soul will rejoice in the LORD and exult in His salvation.
data.commentary_text: net in a pit--or, "pit of their net"--or, "net-pit," as "holy hill" for "hill of holiness" (Psa 2:6); a figure from hunting (Psa 7:15). Their imprecations on impenitent rebels against God need no vindication; His justice and wrath are for such; His mercy for penitents. Compare Psa 7:16; Psa 11:5, on the peculiar fate of the wicked here noticed.

data.verse_text: All my bones will exclaim, “Who is like You, O LORD, who delivers the afflicted from the aggressor, the poor and needy from the robber?”
data.commentary_text:
All my bones--every part. 

him that spoileth him--(Compare Psa 10:2).

data.verse_text: Hostile witnesses come forward; they make charges I know nothing about.
data.commentary_text: False witnesses--literally, "Witnesses of injustice and cruelty" (compare Psa 11:5; Psa 25:19).

data.verse_text: They repay me evil for good, to the bereavement of my soul.
data.commentary_text:
Though they rendered evil for good, he showed a tender sympathy in their affliction. 

spoiling--literally, "bereavement." The usual modes of showing grief are made, as figures, to express his sorrow.

data.verse_text: Yet when they were ill, I put on sackcloth; I humbled myself with fasting, but my prayers returned unanswered.
data.commentary_text: prayer . . . bosom--may denote either the posture--the head bowed--(compare Kg1 18:42) --or, that the prayer was in secret. Some think there is a reference to the result--the prayer would benefit him if not them.

data.verse_text: I paced about as for my friend or brother; I was bowed down with grief, like one mourning for his mother.
data.commentary_text:
behaved--literally, "went on"--denoting his habit. 

heavily--or, "squalidly," his sorrowing occasioning neglect of his person. Altogether, his grief was that of one for a dearly loved relative.

data.verse_text: But when I stumbled, they assembled in glee; they gathered together against me. Assailants I did not know slandered me without ceasing.
data.commentary_text:
On the contrary, they rejoiced in his affliction. Halting, or, "lameness," as in Psa 38:17 for any distress. 

abjects--either as cripples (compare Sa2 4:4), contemptible; or, degraded persons, such as had been beaten (compare Job 30:1-8). 

I knew it not--either the persons, or, reasons of such conduct. 

tear me, and ceased not--literally, "were not silent"--showing that the tearing meant slandering.

data.verse_text: Like godless jesters at a feast, they gnashed their teeth at me.
data.commentary_text: mockers--who were hired to make sport at feasts (Pro 28:21).

data.verse_text: How long, O Lord, will You look on? Rescue my soul from their ravages, my precious life from these lions.
data.commentary_text: darling--(Compare Psa 22:20-21).

data.verse_text: Then I will give You thanks in the great assembly; I will praise You among many people.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Psa 22:22).

data.verse_text: Let not my enemies gloat over me without cause, nor those who hate me without reason wink in malice.
data.commentary_text:
enemies wrongfully--by false and slanderous imputations. 

wink with the eye--an insulting gesture (Pro 6:13). 

without a cause--manifests more malice than having a wrong cause.

data.verse_text: For they do not speak peace, but they devise deceitful schemes against those who live quietly in the land.
data.commentary_text:
deceitful matters--or, "words of deceit." 

quiet in the land--the pious lovers of peace.

data.verse_text: They gape at me and say, “Aha, aha! Our eyes have seen!” O LORD, You have seen it; be not silent. O Lord, be not far from me.
data.commentary_text: On the gesture compare Psa 22:7; and on the expressions of malicious triumph, compare Psa 10:13; Psa 28:3.

data.verse_text: Awake and rise to my defense, to my cause, my God and my Lord! Vindicate me by Your righteousness, O LORD my God, and do not let them gloat over me.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Psa 7:6; Psa 26:1; Th2 1:6). God's righteous government is the hope of the pious and terror of the wicked.

data.verse_text: Let them not say in their hearts, “Aha, just what we wanted!” Let them not say, “We have swallowed him up!”
data.commentary_text: swallowed him up--utterly destroyed him (Psa 21:9; Lam 2:16).

data.verse_text: May those who gloat in my distress be ashamed and confounded; may those who exalt themselves over me be clothed in shame and reproach.
data.commentary_text: clothed--covered wholly (Job 8:22).

data.verse_text: May those who favor my vindication shout for joy and gladness; may they always say, “Exalted be the LORD who delights in His servant’s well-being.”
data.commentary_text:
favour . . . cause--delight in it, as vindicated by Thee. 

Let the Lord, &c.--Let Him be greatly praised for His care of the just.

data.verse_text: Then my tongue will proclaim Your righteousness and Your praises all day long.
data.commentary_text:
In this praise of God's equitable government (Psa 5:8) the writer promises ever to engage.




Next: Psalms Chapter 36

data.verse_text: An oracle is in my heart regarding the transgression of the wicked man: There is no fear of God before his eyes.
data.commentary_text:
On servant of the Lord, see on Psa 18:1, title. The wickedness of man contrasted with the excellency of God's perfections and dispensations; and the benefit of the latter sought, and the evils of the former deprecated. (Psa 36:1-12)

The general sense of this difficult verse is, "that the wicked have no fear of God." The first clause may be rendered, "Saith transgression in my heart, in respect to the wicked, there is no fear," &c., that is, such is my reflection on men's transgressions.

data.verse_text: For his eyes are too full of conceit to detect or hate his own sin. The words of his mouth are wicked and deceitful; he has ceased to be wise and well-doing. Even on his bed he plots wickedness; he sets himself on a path that is not good; he fails to reject evil.
data.commentary_text:
This reflection detailed. 

until his iniquity--literally, "for finding his iniquity for hating"; that is, he persuades himself God will not so find it--"for hating" involving the idea of punishing. Hence his words of iniquity and deceit, and his bold rejection of all right principles of conduct. The climax is that he deliberately adopts and patronizes evil. The negative forms affirm more emphatically their contraries.

data.verse_text: Your loving devotion, O LORD, reaches to the heavens, Your faithfulness to the clouds.
data.commentary_text: mercy . . . and . . . faithfulness--as mercy and truth (Psa 25:10).

data.verse_text: Your righteousness is like the highest mountains; Your judgments are like the deepest sea. O LORD, You preserve man and beast.
data.commentary_text: righteousness [and] judgments--qualities of a good government (Psa 5:8; Psa 31:1). These all are set forth, by the figures used, as unbounded.

data.verse_text: How precious is Your loving devotion, O God, that the children of men take refuge in the shadow of Your wings!
data.commentary_text: shadow of thy wings--(Compare Deu 32:11; Psa 91:1).

data.verse_text: They feast on the abundance of Your house, and You give them drink from Your river of delights.
data.commentary_text:
fatness--richness. 

thy house--residence--for the privileges and blessings of communion with God (Psa 23:6; Psa 27:4). 

river of thy pleasures--plenteous supply; may allude to Eden.

data.verse_text: For with You is the fountain of life; in Your light we see light.
data.commentary_text: Light is an emblem of all blessings, given of God as a means to gain more.

data.verse_text: Extend Your loving devotion to those who know You, and Your righteousness to the upright in heart.
data.commentary_text: that know thee--right knowledge of God is the source of right affections and conduct.

data.verse_text: Let not the foot of the proud come against me, nor the hand of the wicked drive me away.
data.commentary_text: foot of . . . hand . . . wicked--all kinds of violent dealing.

data.verse_text: There the evildoers lie fallen, thrown down and unable to rise.
data.commentary_text:
There--in the acting of violence, they are overthrown. A signal defeat.




Next: Psalms Chapter 37

data.verse_text: Do not fret over those who do evil; do not envy those who do wrong. For they wither quickly like grass and wilt like tender plants.
data.commentary_text:
A composed and uniform trust in God and a constant course of integrity are urged in view of the blessedness of the truly pious, contrasted in various aspects with the final ruin of the wicked. Thus the wisdom and justice of God's providence are vindicated, and its seeming inequalities, which excite the cavils of the wicked and the distrust of the pious, are explained. David's personal history abundantly illustrates the Psalm. (Psa. 37:1-40)

The general sentiment of the whole Psalm is expressed. The righteous need not be vexed by the prosperity of the wicked; for it is transient, and their destiny undesirable.

data.verse_text: Trust in the LORD and do good; dwell in the land and cultivate faithfulness.
data.commentary_text:
Trust--sure of safety. 

shalt thou dwell--or, "dwell thou"; repose quietly. 

verily . . . fed--or, "feed on truth," God's promise (Psa 36:5; compare Hos 12:1).

data.verse_text: Delight yourself in the LORD, and He will give you the desires of your heart.
data.commentary_text: desires-- (Psa 20:5; Psa 21:2), what is lawful and right, really good (Psa 84:11).

data.verse_text: Commit your way to the LORD; trust in Him, and He will do it. He will bring forth your righteousness like the dawn, your justice like the noonday sun.
data.commentary_text:
Commit thy way-- (Pro 16:3). Works--what you have to do and cannot set forth as a burden. 

trust . . . in him--literally, "on Him." He will do what you cannot (compare Psa 22:8; Psa 31:6). He will not suffer your character to remain under suspicion.

data.verse_text: Be still before the LORD and wait patiently for Him; do not fret when men prosper in their ways, when they carry out wicked schemes. Refrain from anger and abandon wrath; do not fret—it can only bring harm.
data.commentary_text:
Rest in--literally, "Be silent to the Lord." 

and wait--Be submissive--avoid petulance and murmurings, anger and rash doing.

data.verse_text: For the evildoers will be cut off, but those who hope in the LORD will inherit the land.
data.commentary_text: Two reasons: The prosperity of the wicked is short; and the pious, by humble trust, will secure all covenant blessing, denoted here by "inherit the earth" (compare Psa 25:13).

data.verse_text: Yet a little while, and the wicked will be no more; though you look for them, they will not be found.
data.commentary_text: shall not be--literally, "is not"--is not to be found.

data.verse_text: But the meek will inherit the land and delight in abundant prosperity.
data.commentary_text: peace--includes prosperity.

data.verse_text: The wicked scheme against the righteous and gnash their teeth at them,
